---
title: "Ch 7 — Linear Inequalities"
subtitle: "Same operations as equations — except multiplying by a negative *flips the sign*"
lesson-id: "ka1.linear-inequalities"
tier: "ka1"
kiselev-book: "Algebra, Part I"
kiselev-chapter: 7
kiselev-chapter-title: "Linear inequalities"
kiselev-sections: "§§113–125 (single inequalities, systems, absolute-value inequalities)"
prerequisites: [ka1.linear-equations]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [research, ai-ml, databases]
estimated-minutes: 40
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you solve $a x + b = 0$ for $x$? See
   [`ka1.linear-equations`](../05-linear-equations/index.qmd).
2. Are signed-arithmetic facts automatic — especially that
   multiplying both sides of an inequality by a *negative* needs a
   sign-flip?
3. Can you locate $-3, 0, +2$ on a number line and compare them by
   "less than"?
4. Are you comfortable with the absolute value $|x|$ and the
   identity $|x| < a \Leftrightarrow -a < x < a$ for $a > 0$?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

A number line. Shade the points $x$ satisfying $x < 3$. Now
multiply both sides by $-1$ — what was a *less-than* becomes a
*greater-than*: $-x > -3$. The same set, written differently.

```{ojs}
viewof a = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 2, step: 1, label: "a"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof b = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: -4, step: 1, label: "b"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  if (a === 0) {
    if (b > 0) return `**0·x + ${b} > 0**: every $x$ works.`;
    if (b === 0) return `**0·x + 0 > 0**: never.`;
    return `**0·x + ${b} > 0**: never.`;
  }
  const flipped = a < 0;
  const x_bound = -b / a;
  const sign = flipped ? "<" : ">";
  return `**${a}x + ${b} > 0**

${a > 0 ? "Solve directly: " : "Multiplying through by 1/a (negative — flip!): "} **x ${sign} ${x_bound.toFixed(3)}**.

${flipped ? "*Notice the inequality flipped because a < 0.*" : "*No flip — a > 0.*"}`;
}
```

The single new rule: **multiplying both sides by a negative
reverses the inequality**.

## Wonder

1. The inequality $x < 5$ has *infinitely many* solutions. Is that
   a problem? How do we describe such a solution set?
2. Why does multiplying by a *negative* reverse the inequality?
   *(Try $-2 < 3$, multiply both sides by $-1$.)*
3. Two inequalities $x > 2$ AND $x < 5$ — what does the *combined*
   solution look like?
4. The inequality $|x - 3| < 2$ — how does this look on the number
   line?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Solution set is an *interval* (or union of intervals). We
describe it by a *bound* or by *interval notation*: $(-\infty, 5)$.
(2) $-2 < 3$ becomes $2 > -3$ — yes, the *order* on the number line
is *reversed* by the reflection $x \mapsto -x$. (3) The
*intersection*: $2 < x < 5$, written as the open interval $(2, 5)$.
(4) An interval centred at $3$ of half-width $2$: $(1, 5)$.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — A simple inequality

Solve $3 x - 7 > 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 x > 12 \Rightarrow x > 4$. **Solution: $x > 4$, or $(4, \infty)$.**
</details>

### Problem 2 — Multiplying by a negative

Solve $-2 x + 3 \le 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Subtract $3$, then divide by $-2$ — *flip the sign*.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-2 x \le 6 \Rightarrow x \ge -3$. **Solution: $x \ge -3$, or
$[-3, \infty)$.**

(*The flip happens at the divide-by-$-2$ step.*)
</details>

### Problem 3 — Variable on both sides

Solve $5 x - 2 < 2 x + 7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 x < 9 \Rightarrow x < 3$. **$(-\infty, 3)$.**
</details>

### Problem 4 — Compound inequality

Solve $-3 < 2 x - 1 \le 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Add $1$ throughout, then divide by $2$ throughout.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-2 < 2 x \le 6 \Rightarrow -1 < x \le 3$. **$(-1, 3]$.**
</details>

### Problem 5 — Absolute-value inequality

Solve $|2 x - 1| < 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|y| < 5 \Leftrightarrow -5 < y < 5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-5 < 2 x - 1 < 5 \Rightarrow -4 < 2 x < 6 \Rightarrow -2 < x < 3$.
**$(-2, 3)$.**
</details>

### Problem 6 — Reversed absolute value

Solve $|x + 2| > 3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|y| > 3 \Leftrightarrow y > 3$ OR $y < -3$ — a *union*, not an
*intersection*.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x + 2 > 3$ OR $x + 2 < -3$, i.e. $x > 1$ or $x < -5$. **$(-\infty,
-5) \cup (1, \infty)$.**
</details>

### Problem 7 — System of inequalities

Solve simultaneously: $\begin{cases} 2 x + 3 > 5 \\ -x + 4 \ge 0
\end{cases}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
First: $x > 1$. Second: $x \le 4$. Both: $1 < x \le 4$, **$(1,
4]$.**
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The symbols **<** and **>** are surprisingly young. They were
introduced by the English mathematician **Thomas Harriot** in his
posthumously published *Artis analyticae praxis* (1631). Harriot
chose them to mimic the *opening* of an angle — wider on the side
of the larger quantity. The non-strict siblings $\le$ and $\ge$ came
later, designed by the French astronomer **Pierre Bouguer** in 1734.
Algebra had been around for nearly a millennium by the time these
five tiny symbols arrived; before them, mathematicians wrote
"is less than" out in full, every time.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**A [linear inequality](/glossary.qmd#linear-inequality) is a linear
equation with one new rule: multiplying or dividing by a negative
flips the relation.** Why? Because reflection across zero — the map
$x \mapsto -x$ — *reverses the order* on the number line. Everything
else is identical to Chapter 5: move terms, combine, divide. The
solution is no longer a single point but a *half-line, interval, or
union* — the [feasible region](/glossary.qmd#feasible-region) of one
constraint.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Linear inequality:** any of $a x + b < 0, > 0, \le 0, \ge 0$.
The solution is a half-line (or a union, or all of $\mathbb R$,
depending on degeneracy).

**Operations preserving the solution set.**

1. Add (or subtract) the same expression to both sides.
2. Multiply (or divide) both sides by a *positive* constant.
3. **Multiply (or divide) by a negative — and *flip the
   inequality***. *(This is the only new rule.)*

**Three-step solving.**

- Move all $x$-terms to one side, constants to the other.
- Combine.
- Divide by the coefficient of $x$. Flip if the coefficient is
  negative.

**Compound and absolute-value forms.**

- $a < f(x) < b$ — solve as a *both-sides* compound.
- $|f(x)| < a$, $a > 0$ — equivalent to $-a < f(x) < a$.
- $|f(x)| > a$, $a > 0$ — equivalent to $f(x) > a$ OR $f(x) < -a$
  (a *union*).

:::

A verse:

> *Same operations as equations, but watch the negative multiplier.*
> *Flip the inequality, every time — that is the rule, no
> requalifier.*
> *And the absolute value gives a band: less-than is "between",*
> *Greater-than is "outside" — two outcomes, one routine.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Solve $2 |x - 1| - 3 \le 1$.

Add $3$: $2 |x - 1| \le 4$. Divide by $2$: $|x - 1| \le 2$.
This is $-2 \le x - 1 \le 2$, i.e. $-1 \le x \le 3$.

**$[-1, 3]$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Solve $4 x + 5 > 13$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4 x > 8 \Rightarrow x > 2$.
</details>

**W2.** Solve $-3 x \le 12$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x \ge -4$ (flip!).
</details>

**W3.** Solve $|x| < 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-4 < x < 4$, **$(-4, 4)$**.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Solve $\dfrac{x - 2}{3} > \dfrac{x + 1}{4}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply both sides by $12$ (positive — no flip).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4(x - 2) > 3 (x + 1) \Rightarrow 4 x - 8 > 3 x + 3 \Rightarrow
x > 11$.
</details>

**P2.** Solve $|3 x - 1| \ge 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|y| \ge 2 \Leftrightarrow y \ge 2$ or $y \le -2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 x - 1 \ge 2$ ($x \ge 1$) or $3 x - 1 \le -2$ ($x \le -1/3$).
**$(-\infty, -1/3] \cup [1, \infty)$.**
</details>

**P3.** A parking lot charges $₹50$ for the first hour and $₹20$
per additional hour. With $₹250$, what is the maximum time you
can park (an integer hours)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let $h$ = hours. Cost: $50 + 20(h - 1) \le 250$ for $h \ge 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$50 + 20 h - 20 \le 250 \Rightarrow 20 h \le 220 \Rightarrow h \le
11$. **$11$ hours.**
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Solve $\dfrac{x - 1}{x + 2} > 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A fraction is positive iff numerator and denominator have the
*same sign*.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
*Both positive:* $x > 1$ AND $x > -2$ — i.e. $x > 1$.
*Both negative:* $x < 1$ AND $x < -2$ — i.e. $x < -2$.
**Solution: $x < -2$ or $x > 1$, i.e. $(-\infty, -2) \cup (1,
\infty)$.**

(*Always exclude points where the denominator is zero — here,
$x = -2$.*)
</details>

**S2.** Show that $|a + b| \le |a| + |b|$ for all real $a, b$.
*(The triangle inequality.)*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Square both sides — both are non-negative, so squaring preserves
the inequality.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|a + b|^2 = (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$.
$(|a| + |b|)^2 = a^2 + 2|a||b| + b^2$.

Difference: $(|a| + |b|)^2 - |a + b|^2 = 2 (|a||b| - ab) =
2 (|ab| - ab) \ge 0$ (since $|ab| \ge ab$ for all real $ab$).

So $(|a| + |b|)^2 \ge |a + b|^2$. Take square roots (both
non-negative): $|a + b| \le |a| + |b|$. **QED.** *(Algebra I §122
— the *triangle inequality* in absolute-value form, named after
the 2D triangle inequality.)*
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's terse style, adapted from
*Algebra I*, §§118–125.

**K1.** Find the smallest integer $x$ satisfying $\dfrac{2 x - 5}{3}
> \dfrac{x + 1}{4}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Multiply by $12$: $4(2 x - 5) > 3(x + 1)$, i.e. $8 x - 20 > 3 x +
3$, so $5 x > 23$, $x > \dfrac{23}{5} = 4.6$. **Smallest integer:
$x = 5$.**
</details>

**K2.** Show that for all positive $a, b, c$, $\dfrac{a}{b + c} +
\dfrac{b}{c + a} + \dfrac{c}{a + b} \ge \dfrac{3}{2}$. *(Nesbitt's
inequality.)*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Add $1$ to each fraction: $\dfrac{a + b + c}{b + c}$, etc. The
sum becomes $(a + b + c)(\dfrac{1}{b + c} + \dfrac{1}{c + a} +
\dfrac{1}{a + b})$. Apply AM-HM.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $s = a + b + c$. Adding $3$ to LHS: $\dfrac{a + b + c}{b + c}
+ \dfrac{a + b + c}{c + a} + \dfrac{a + b + c}{a + b} = s
\left(\dfrac{1}{b + c} + \dfrac{1}{c + a} + \dfrac{1}{a + b}\right)$.

By **AM-HM** (or Cauchy-Schwarz): $\dfrac{1}{b + c} + \dfrac{1}
{c + a} + \dfrac{1}{a + b} \ge \dfrac{9}{(b + c) + (c + a) +
(a + b)} = \dfrac{9}{2 s}$.

So LHS + 3 $\ge s \cdot \dfrac{9}{2 s} = \dfrac{9}{2}$, i.e.
LHS $\ge \dfrac{3}{2}$. **QED.** *(Nesbitt 1903; a celebrated
3-variable inequality.)*
</details>

**K3.** For what real $x$ is $\dfrac{1}{x - 1} + \dfrac{1}{x + 1} >
0$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Combine: $\dfrac{2 x}{x^2 - 1}$. Sign analysis on numerator and
denominator.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{2 x}{x^2 - 1} > 0$ iff $2 x$ and $x^2 - 1$ have the same
sign.

$x^2 - 1 > 0$ iff $|x| > 1$, i.e. $x > 1$ or $x < -1$.
$x^2 - 1 < 0$ iff $-1 < x < 1$.
$2 x > 0$ iff $x > 0$. $2 x < 0$ iff $x < 0$.

Same sign:
- Both positive: $x > 1$.
- Both negative: $-1 < x < 0$.

(Exclude $x = \pm 1$.) **Solution: $-1 < x < 0$ or $x > 1$.**
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Kiselev, *Алгебра, Ч.1*
(Fizmatlit reprint 2006), §§113–125. Russian original:
[archive.org scan](https://archive.org/details/1_kiselev_algebra).
The triangle inequality (S2) is §122; Nesbitt's inequality (K2) is
a 20th-century classic that fits Kiselev's style.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Multiplying by a negative is the same as multiplying by a
   positive."** *No.* Multiplying by a negative *reverses* the
   inequality. This is the single most-often-violated rule in
   inequality manipulation.
2. **"$|x| < a$ and $|x| > a$ have the same shape."** Opposite
   shapes! $|x| < a$ is an interval (between); $|x| > a$ is a
   union of two rays (outside).
3. **"You can solve a fraction-inequality by clearing denominators."**
   Only with care — multiplying by a denominator whose sign you
   don't know can flip the inequality. Better: combine into one
   fraction and analyse signs (S1, K3).
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

1. **One new rule, one old engine.** Inequalities use the same three
   moves as equations — except *flip the relation* whenever you
   multiply or divide both sides by a negative. Internalise the flip
   and inequalities collapse into a familiar drill.
2. **Less-than-band, greater-than-outside.** $|f(x)| < a$ confines
   $f$ to an interval; $|f(x)| > a$ pushes $f$ to a *union of two
   rays*. The shapes are opposites — one bounded, one unbounded — and
   the difference comes from the geometry of distance.
3. **Solutions are sets, not points.** The output of an inequality is
   an interval, a union of intervals, or sometimes all of $\mathbb R$
   — never a finite list. Get fluent with interval notation; that's
   the language for describing the answer.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the *flip rule* for multiplying inequalities by
   negatives, and explain *why* it's true?
2. Did the absolute-value inequalities (Problems 5 and 6) feel
   like the same template applied twice (interval vs union)?
3. Can you set up a real-world inequality (parking, budget, dosage)
   from a verbal description?

- **3 yes:** move on to *Powers and Roots* (Ch 8).
- **2 yes:** redo Problem 6 — the union form is the chapter's
  trickiest pattern.
- **0–1 yes:** practise W1, W2, W3 with several variations until
  the flip becomes automatic.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`ka1.linear-equations`](../05-linear-equations/index.qmd) — same
  manipulation, with one new rule (the flip).

### What this unlocks

- **`ka1.powers-roots`** *(Ch 8, planned)* — square roots take
  non-negative arguments only — a *domain inequality* in disguise.
- **`ka1.quadratic-equations`** *(Ch 9, planned)* — solving
  quadratic *inequalities* via sign-analysis, generalising K3.
- [`ka2.higher-degree-inequalities`](../../tier-kiselev-2-algebra-2/index.qmd) *(Algebra II, planned)* —
  the chapter on higher-degree inequalities directly extends this
  one.
- [`t6.convex-optimization`](../../tier-6-undergrad-advanced/09-convex-optimization/index.qmd) —
  feasibility regions are intersections of inequality constraints.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Algebra, Part I — Chapter 7: Linear inequalities (§§113–125).**
  Russian original on
  [archive.org](https://archive.org/details/1_kiselev_algebra).
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t2.linear-inequalities`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/03-linear-inequalities/index.qmd)
  and
  [`cbse11.linear-inequalities`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/05-linear-inequalities/index.qmd).
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full development
  of *systems of inequalities* and the *graphical method*
  (§§120–125).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** **Constrained optimisation** — find the minimum of an
objective subject to inequality constraints — is the workhorse of
operations research and modern ML training. The *feasibility
region* is the intersection of half-spaces, exactly as set up in
this chapter.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Every **range query** (`age > 18 AND age < 65`) is
a system of linear inequalities applied to indexed columns. Query
planners use the same combine-and-simplify rules.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** **Linear programming** — minimise a linear objective
over a system of linear inequalities — is the gateway to
*operations research*, used everywhere from airline scheduling to
diet planning. The simplex method literally walks the boundary of
the feasibility region.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the §§113–125 ordering); Nesbitt (the
  inequality in K2); the AM-HM inequality (used implicitly in K2).
- **The flip rule** is the chapter's only genuinely new content
  beyond Ch 5; everything else is *operational mirror* of linear
  equations. Lock it in.
- **What this lesson does not do:** treat the *Cauchy-Schwarz
  inequality* in full. That belongs in undergraduate analysis or
  Algebra II.
:::
